entry.title: SSO Migration from ADFS to Entra ID. IAS – Guidance Request
entry.content:
Hello,

We are planning to migrate our corporate Identity Provider from ADFS to Microsoft Entra ID for SSO authentication. SuccessFactors (including LMS), using IAS. At this stage, we are in the planning phase and would like to begin with the LMS Preview environment before proceeding to Production. 

System / Landscape:

  • Product: SAP SuccessFactors (Learning)
  • Identity Layer: SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS/IPS)
  • Current Corporate IdP: ADFS
  • Planning Corporate IdP: Microsoft Entra ID
  • Environments: LMS Preview (Test) and LMS Production

We would appreciate guidance on the recommended approach to configure Entra ID as a new Corporate IdP in IAS alongside the existing ADFS.

Thank you,

Serguei,

entry.title: Re: Crystal Reports and .Net Core
entry.content:
The short answer, afaik, is no.
If you search this forum, you'll find the same question has been answered multiple times.
Bottom line: stay on .NET 4.81 or lower (or delegate from your .NET Core to a .NET 4.81 just for the Crystal aspect).

entry.title: Somos Comunidade SAP Curitiba – Fabiele Boico
entry.content:
Este post faz parte da série  “Somos SAP Community Curitiba”  , criada para compartilhar histórias de pessoas que ajudam a construir e fortalecer o ecossistema SAP em nossa cidade.

A ideia da série foi apresentada neste post de abertura:  Introdução - Série da comunidade: Somos SAP Community Curitiba

A proposta é simples: responder algumas perguntas e contar um pouco da nossa jornada dentro da comunidade.

Fui convidado por @SimoneAzevedo para participar da série e compartilhar um pouco da minha jornada no ecossistema SAP. Você pode conferir o post dela aqui:  Somos SAP Community Curitiba – Simone Azevedo - SAP Community   


🚀Quem sou eu na Comunidade SAP Curitiba?

Eu sou aquela que 'deu o match' perfeito com a Simone e acabou sendo fisgada por esse ecossistema fantástico! Brincadeiras à parte, hoje atuo como Head de Marketing na CVA . Entrei na comunidade de mansinho e acabei virando fã de carteirinha (e patrocinadores!). Estar presente em eventos como o SAP Inside Track Curitiba e o SAP Talk Women foi um divisor de águas; não foi apenas marketing, foi um 'achado' que já faz parte da história oficial da CVA. Estamos em casa!

❤️O que mais me motiva a continuar fazendo parte da SAP Community Curitiba?

O que me mantém aqui é uma troca real . No marketing, falamos muito sobre conexões, mas aqui na SAP Community a gente vive isso na prática. É a energia de compartilhar conhecimento, as conversas de corredor que viram insights valiosos e, claro, ver que sempre tem alguém disposto a ajudar. É impossível não querer voltar para o próximo evento!

✨Como fazer parte da comunidade impactou minha jornada profissional ou pessoal?

Profissionalmente, você ganha uma visibilidade incrível e um reconhecimento que só uma comunidade unida proporciona. Pessoalmente? Tem sido um deslumbre! Eu vim de outras áreas e, para mim, o ‘universo SAP’ ainda brilha com o frescor da novidade. Me sinto um eterno aprendizado com brilho nos olhos. Sou imensamente grato por esse recomeço e por ter mentores e parceiros de jornada como o Juliano Kisielewicz, o Jean Struck , o Rodrigo Okita Yamamoto e o Edson Alves . Sem eles, essa aventura não seria tão rica!

SAP Inside Track 2025 - Time CVASAP Talk Women 2026

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

❓Que conselho você daria para quem está começando agora no ecossistema SAP e ainda não conhece a comunidade?

Não tenha medo do novo. O ecossistema SAP é gigante, mas a comunidade Curitiba é o quintal onde a gente aprende a jogar junto. Venha pelo conhecimento, fique pelas pessoas!

🌐Conecte-se comigo:  https://www.linkedin.com/in/fabiele-cristine-boico-2473a529/

 

💫Convidado para continuar a série


Para continuar a série Somos SAP Community Curitiba, convido:  @Ironcva2025para compartilhar sua história e experiências na comunidade.

Além das perguntas da série:
1.  Quem sou eu na SAP Community Curitiba?
2.  O que mais me motiva a continuar fazendo parte do SAP Community Curitiba?
3.  Como fazer parte da comunidade impactou minha jornada profissional ou pessoal?

Deixo também a pergunta adicional: 

Que conselho você daria para alguém que está começando agora no ecossistema SAP e ainda não conhece a comunidade?

 

Frase final

E assim sigo, com o coração grato e a mentalidade sempre pronto para o próximo evento. A Simone abriu as portas, eu entrei com tudo, e agora quero saber: quem é o próximo a contar sua história? ✨

 

#SomosSAPCommunityCWB
#SAPCommunityCWB
#SAPCommunity

entry.title: Transporting OAuth Scope for v4 OData service /IWFND/V4_ADMIN
entry.content:
I have created an OData v4 service binding for my RAP service.  I can use transaction /IWFND/V4_ADMIN to publish my service and include it in a customizing request.  I create the required OAuth scope. When I use transaction SCC1N to copy my transport to another client my service is published there.  This is great because it means that my service will be published in downstream systems when the transport is imported.

My issue is with the OAuth scope. I don't see how to include this in a transport, and when I go to the 2nd client I have to create the OAuth scope again.

What is the correct procedure?  Is there a way to transport the OAuth scope, or will I have to create it in all the downstream systems?

I have reviewed SAP Help and can't find an answer to this.  Ditto with KBAs like 3304204 & 3605323.  Any assistance much appreciated

S/4HANA Private Cloud 2023 FPS03

entry.title: Re: Limit in SAP Cloud ALM on the number of System Groups...
entry.content:
You can create multiple system groups for a product (e.g., main line system group, project line system group, and so on) and create multiple deployment plans.
You can assign a deployment plan (with multiple system groups and releases) to multiple projects. However, a project can have only one deployment plan.

The deployment plan considers the transport routes configured at the Transport Management System (TMS) level on the managed system, not just the system group structure.

The base memory size of all SAP Cloud ALM tenants is 24 GB.

A large number of created features will add to storage usage.
The uploaded external documents related to features are stored in the SAP BTP DMS repository.

The main factors impacting memory usage are configured retention times for monitoring data and the amount of data generated by operations monitoring
(e.g., Health, Integration, and Exception Monitoring).
You may want to review the report /SDF/CALM_MEMORY_ESTIMATION, which provides a workload‑based method for estimating the memory requirements of SAP Cloud ALM.

entry.title: Custom exchange rate type cannot be used for translation from doc currency(00) to CC currency 10
entry.content:
Dear Team, 

After creating and testing a new custom exchange rate type, we observed that the new exchange rate type cannot be used for translation from document currency (type 00) to company code currency (type 10). 

Reviewing the configuration in SSCUI 103379 'Define Currency Settings for Ledgers and Company Codes', the option to change exchange rate type from M to the new custom type is not available for 00 --> 10 translation. 

As a result, the system continues to use exchange rate type M for document currency --> company code currency translation. We have a business requirement to us the new custom exchange rate type for 00 --> 10 translation. 


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Custom exchange rate type cannot be used for translat...
entry.content:
It is not possible in the system to change the default exchange rate type = M for the currency conversion from document currency (00) to company code currency (10). This option is greyed out in the configuration. 

As a workaround you could consider to use the procedure provided in the following article as a means of achieving the desired result: 

Currency Conversion with Prescribed Exchange Rates

entry.title: How to avoid displaying Service Calls without any Activity on the Planning List
entry.content:
Service Calls without any activities inside them should not be seen on Planning List

How can we achieve this?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How to avoid displaying Service Calls without any Act...
entry.content:
Read Model Filter in User Policy Group with below Query can be used for achieving the expected Behavior:

{
    "query": {
        "bool": {
            "must": [
                {
                  "exists": {
                    "field": "FsmPlanningListItem.activity.id"
                  }
                }
            ]
        }
    }
}

entry.title: Master Data for Origin Acceptance at Vendor Premises - Valuated Stock in Transit - Movement type 107
entry.content:
To default of the indicator in the PO, we may find that there are multiple options, which is not true. 
This section covers all the data elements applicable for 'Origin Acceptance' indicator and the right one to enable defaulting to PO.
 1. Material Master: The MARA-WEORA field can be enabled in the material master on the purchasing view under the section 'other data/manufacturer data.'
Configuration: Navigate to SPRO → Logistics - General → Material Master → Configuring the Material Master → Define Structure of Data Screens for Each Screen Sequence (OMT3). Identify the screen sequence assigned to your material type, which is generally 21 in the standard delivered content. Select screen sequence 21 and screen 14 for purchasing, and navigate to the sub-screen section and replace sub-screen (4) 2313 with 2314. Screen 2314 will enable the field MARA-WEORA in the material master.
Note: This field is only used to transfer data to third-party systems. it will not help in auto-populating the indicator in any sourcing documents, such as the purchase order, contract, or purchase info-records.

2. Vendor Master (BP)
On the BP supplier role, vendor general data tab, in the additional general data section, 'Origin Acceptance' (LFA1-WEORA) can be found. If the indicator is set, it defaults to the purchase order for all the materials supplied by this vendor. To automatically populate the indicator on the PO, this is the only option available without any configuration or customization.
Note: The indicator on the BP will auto-populate on the PO for all the items.

3. Purchase order Item: On the PO item delivery tab, enable the EKPO-WEORA field.
Configuration: Navigate to SPRO → MM → Purchasing → Purchase Order → Define Screen Layout at Document Level --> Select appropriate Field Selection key → GR/IR Control → Configure Origin Acceptance as optional entry (if you don't find the field, use page down option to find the field)
Note: By activating this field, we will be able to see the checked indicator on the PO. If this field is inactive, the vendor master indicator updates the EKPO table. This is the only field we need to configure to manage the PO manually.

4. Contract (ME31K) has this field available to record at the item level in the section ‘GR/IR control.
Note: Though there is a provision to update the indicator in the contract at the item level, unfortunately, this field is not designed to automatically copy over to the PO. To copy this indicator over to PO, use BAdI ME_PROCESS_PO_CUST (method PROCESS_ITEM). 

5. It is important to note that there is no provision to record this indicator in the purchase info records.

Summary: To auto-populate the EKPO-WEORA value in the purchase order, we have 2 options. Either use the vendor master or use BAdI to copy the data from the contract to the Purchase Order.

For more details, refer to SAP note https://me.sap.com/notes/1875263.

entry.title: Unable to track Parked Invoice details
entry.content:
We are looking for a way to track invoices that transition from a parked to a posted status. We have observed that while parked invoices contain full details regarding the user and timestamp, this information is updated with the posting details once the process is complete.
We reviewed SAP Note 2693589 (Version 1, Released 13.09.2018) regarding FB03/MIR7 parking information. Since we are on SAPS4Core 108, these changes should already be reflected in our system.
Is there a more recent SAP Note regarding this issue? If not, could you please advise on the best way to track changes and maintain visibility of the original parking details after an invoice has been posted?

entry.title: CAP, RAP, or Both: Stop Hiding Behind 'It Depends'
entry.content:
I've read every major article on this topic. They all end the same way:

"Both models are complementary. Choose based on your context. Use each for the right scenario."

That is a technically accurate, professionally safe, and completely useless answer.

This article gives you the framework I actually use with clients — including the uncomfortable parts most posts skip: the staffing bias that impact architecture decisions, what to do when your client has thousands of Z-objects, why migrating RAP to CAP is not a refactor, and why CAP is the only model that enforces the Clean Core promise architecturally.

One thing upfront: this is not an anti-ABAP article. ABAP knowledge — deep understanding of business processes, transactional models, and SAP data structures accumulated over years — is one of the most valuable assets in enterprise

What We're Actually Debating

RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) is SAP's extension model for the S/4HANA core and ABAP Cloud environments. It's ABAP-native, optimized for extending standard SAP Business Objects with transactional consistency. SAP's answer to "how do I extend S/4HANA without modifying standard code."

CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model) is SAP's framework for building cloud-native services on SAP BTP. Node.js and Java, BTP Cloud Foundry and Kyma, multitenancy, eventing, and OData/GraphQL out of the box. SAP's answer to "how do I build new business capabilities that live outside the S/4HANA core."

Same goal — Clean Core, BTP-native development. Fundamentally different execution contexts.

The Uncomfortable Truth First

Most teams choosing between CAP and RAP are not making a pure architectural decision.

They're making a decision shaped by team composition.

An ABAP-heavy organization will present RAP as the natural choice for almost any BTP extension. The justification will sound architectural: "We need access to S/4HANA data," "We want to leverage existing ABAP logic," "RAP is Clean Core approved." All of those statements can be true and still be a rationalization. The real driver is often simpler: the team knows ABAP, so RAP wins.

This is a systemic pattern worth naming explicitly — because when staffing drives architecture, the decisions look reasonable one at a time and catastrophic in aggregate. The job of a principal architect is to separate these two questions before the decision is made.

When RAP Is Genuinely the Right Answer

1. Extending Standard SAP Business Objects

If you need to extend standard S/4HANA entities — Sales Orders, Purchase Requisitions, Business Partners, Material Documents — RAP is purpose-built for this. Proper lock handling, transactional consistency, draft management, clean integration with standard SAP workflows. Trying to replicate this via CAP calling S/4HANA APIs introduces latency, complexity, and consistency gaps that are difficult to manage at scale. For tight S/4HANA core extensions, RAP's native access is a genuine advantage, not just familiarity.

2. On-Premise + Cloud Parity Requirements

RAP runs on both on-premise ABAP Platform and ABAP Cloud. CAP is cloud-only. Hybrid deployment, phased cloud migration, regulated industries with on-premise constraints — RAP is the only viable option when parity is required.

3. Maximizing Existing ABAP Investment

Complex, well-tested ABAP business logic that needs to be exposed as a service can be wrapped with a Clean Core API facade in RAP without rewriting it. Legitimate — with the caveat covered in the Z section below.

4. Simple, Bounded In-App Extensions

A single-source, single-consumer extension with no external integrations and no scalability concerns does not need the infrastructure overhead of CAP. Don't over-engineer.

When CAP Is the Right Answer — Which Is More Often Than You Think

1. Multi-Source Data Scenarios

The moment your extension needs data from more than one system, CAP is the correct choice. CAP's service layer was designed for data federation. RAP operating across multiple external sources becomes a manual integration exercise.

2. Independent Scalability

Side-by-side BTP extensions with variable load profiles should not be constrained by ABAP runtime characteristics. CAP on BTP Cloud Foundry or Kyma scales horizontally.

3. Talent Pool and Long-Term Maintainability

The global Node.js and Java developer pool is orders of magnitude larger than ABAP Cloud specialists. But the real point is different: the scarce resource in SAP programs is not syntax knowledge — it's SAP business process knowledge. An architecture that requires ABAP Cloud specialists to maintain it creates organizational dependency risk. An ABAP architect who already has the process knowledge and adds CAP to their toolkit is the most valuable person in the room.

4. Multi-Tenant SaaS and Reusable Services

CAP's multitenancy model is production-grade out of the box. RAP was not designed for this.

5. Non-SAP Ecosystem Integration

When your extension lives at the intersection of SAP and non-SAP systems, CAP's polyglot nature is a structural advantage. Building this in RAP means engineering brittle ABAP-to-external-API bridges.

6. Survival of SAP Upgrade Cycles

CAP extensions communicate with S/4HANA via stable, versioned APIs. They have no ABAP lifecycle. This connects directly to the Clean Core argument below.

The Big Z Problem

Most clients don't arrive at this debate with a clean slate. They arrive with 1,000, 2,000, sometimes 5,000 Z-objects accumulated over decades of ECC customization. The first proposal is almost always: "Let's migrate the Zs to RAP."

This is the wrong answer — and it is expensive.

The right question, before any migration decision, is: why does each Z-object exist?

Category 1 — SAP now covers it natively (retire it)
A significant portion of Z-objects from ECC exist because SAP didn't have that functionality in 2005. S/4HANA and BTP may have it natively today. Before migrating anything, check whether SAP standard now covers the need. If it does: retire the Z. Don't migrate what you should delete. 

Category 2 — Genuine core business object extension (evaluate RAP)
Some Z-objects encode real business logic tightly coupled to SAP transactional objects — custom validations on Sales Orders, derivations on Financial documents, extensions to MM flows. These are legitimate RAP candidates because the coupling is intentional and the transactional integrity requirement is real.

Category 3 — Side-by-side logic that never belonged in the core (evaluate CAP with bias)
Many Z-objects are reporting utilities, data extraction routines, integration helpers, or workflow logic placed inside the ABAP stack out of convenience, not architecture. These should be evaluated for CAP first — finally living where they should have lived from the beginning. One qualifier: if the logic requires HANA-native pushdown on large datasets (financial close, ATP, margin analysis at scale), or participates in SAP's transactional determination chain, evaluate before rebuilding. The default is still CAP. The exception needs justification.

The Big Z is not a RAP migration backlog. It's a forcing function to finally answer which of your customizations your business actually needs, which SAP now covers natively, and which were architectural accidents waiting to be corrected.

And for the Zs that survive the triage but can't be rewritten this quarter — wrap them. A clean API boundary around legacy logic is not technical debt; it's the first act of modernization. What you cannot afford is leaving Z-code directly exposed. That's how "temporary" becomes permanent.

The CAP First Decision Protocol

CAP First means the burden of proof shifts. The default is CAP. RAP must earn its place by satisfying a specific gate.

The only gate that stops CAP: transactional consistency with a standard SAP Business Object, bounded to S/4HANA data, with no external systems, no multi-tenancy, and no independent scalability requirements.

One honest prerequisite: CAP First is the right posture for organizations that have already invested in BTP platform maturity — an operational subaccount, CI/CD for Node.js or Java, and the operational capability for Cloud Foundry or Kyma workloads. For organizations that haven't built that foundation yet, CAP First is still the right destination — it comes with a deliberate enablement journey first. The posture doesn't change. The timeline does.

Why does the default posture matter? Because the cost of the wrong decision is asymmetric.

The Real Cost of Getting the Model Wrong

A clarification worth making explicitly, because ABAP architects will correctly challenge a weaker version of this argument:

Upgrading S/4HANA with existing RAP extensions is relatively low cost — if your RAP uses only ABAP Cloud Released APIs (C1), which is what Clean Core RAP should do. SAP guarantees stability on released APIs across versions. ABAP teams who make this point are correct.

The cost argument I'm making is different. It's about switching models — what happens when you built in RAP and later discover that CAP was the right choice: multi-source data appeared, scalability became a requirement, the business needs this as a multi-tenant service.

RAP is ABAP. CAP is Node.js or Java. These are not different versions of the same language — different runtimes, different deployment models, different ecosystems entirely. When you migrate a RAP service to CAP:

  • The CDS data model needs to be rewritten in CAP CDS (similar syntax, different runtime behavior)
  • All business logic needs to be reimplemented in Node.js or Java — not ported, reimplemented
  • The deployment pipeline, CI/CD, monitoring, and operational model all change
  • The ABAP artifacts are retired, not converted

For a medium-complexity service: weeks to months. For a large one: a quarter or more. For a portfolio that grew because nobody pushed back: a program, not a project.

The cost of the wrong default is asymmetric. Not because RAP upgrades are expensive — they're not. But because choosing the wrong model and needing to switch is. CAP First reduces the risk of landing on that side.

One variable that changes this equation: AI. Claude Code connected to SAP ADT via MCP can analyze RAP logic and generate CAP equivalents faster than before — it lowers the code generation cost. It does not replace the authorization model revalidation, the test suite rebuild, or the operational cutover, which is where most of the real effort lives. The fear factor drops. The effort doesn't disappear. That full conversation is a follow-up article.

CAP Enforces the Clean Core Promise. RAP Requires Discipline to Maintain It.

SAP's official definition of Clean Core: don't modify standard code, use the approved extensibility models. RAP is on that list — and a RAP extension using only ABAP Cloud Released APIs (C1) is genuinely Clean Core compliant by SAP's own definition. Let's be precise about this.

But Clean Core has a purpose beyond compliance: the ability to upgrade your SAP core without touching your business logic. Faster upgrades. Lower risk. Smaller impact windows.

Here is the real distinction between RAP and CAP on this point — and it is not about compliance. It is about enforcement.

Both models can satisfy the Clean Core compliance definition when implemented with discipline. The difference is what happens when discipline breaks down. In RAP, a team under delivery pressure can find paths to unreleased ABAP internals — friend class patterns, dynamic calls, legacy function module invocations. The architectural boundary exists as a rule, not a wall. In CAP, you are physically outside the ABAP stack. You cannot access unreleased internals because there is no path to them from a Node.js or Java runtime. The boundary is enforced by the architecture itself.

RAP requires discipline to stay Clean Core. CAP makes it structurally difficult to leave.

This is not a criticism of ABAP developers. It is a recognition that architectural guardrails matter most under pressure — and pressure is the normal operating condition of enterprise SAP programs.

A valid counterpoint here: ABAP Cloud on BTP — the ABAP Environment as a side-by-side deployment — does break the S/4HANA lifecycle dependency. The toolchain enforces Released APIs syntactically via the language version check, so the "discipline" argument partially weakens. But there is a distinction worth making: ABAP Cloud on BTP decouples you from S/4HANA's runtime lifecycle. CAP decouples you from the ABAP architecture itself. If your constraints are about upgrade survival, both work. If your constraints are about talent pool, integration patterns, and long-term architectural independence away from the ABAP stack — only one of them gets you there.

A CAP service communicates with S/4HANA through stable, versioned OData or API endpoints. It does not know what version of S/4HANA is running underneath. It has no ABAP lifecycle. It can be upgraded, redeployed, and scaled completely independently. RAP is the right direction. CAP is the destination. The difference is whether Clean Core survives contact with reality.

This Is Not Anti-ABAP. It's a Call to Deploy ABAP Knowledge Differently.

Deep ABAP expertise — understanding transactional models, business process flows, SAP data structures built over decades — is irreplaceable. The scarce resource in SAP programs is not syntax knowledge. It is SAP business process knowledge. A Node.js developer learns the CAP framework in weeks. They do not learn how SAP processes an intercompany stock transfer in weeks. ABAP architects already have the hard part.

The problem is not ABAP knowledge. The problem is when that knowledge becomes a reason to avoid CAP rather than a foundation for it.

In 2026, with Claude Code connected to SAP ADT via MCP and Joule for Developers embedded in the SAP ecosystem, an experienced ABAP developer can build a production-grade CAP service with AI assistance in a fraction of the time it would have taken two years ago. The language gap has never been smaller to bridge. The "we don't have CAP developers" argument has never been weaker.

And the most mature SAP architectures don't choose one model. They use both deliberately: RAP as the API layer of S/4HANA — the clean, stable interface that exposes core business objects. CAP as the orchestration and composition layer above it — multi-source, scalable, independently deployable. That is not a compromise. That is the target state. ABAP architects who understand both are positioned to design it.

A Challenge for Your Next Architecture Review

Before your team decides on RAP — or commits to a Z-to-RAP migration program — answer four questions publicly in the room:

  1. For Z-objects: Does each Z exist because SAP lacked functionality that it now has natively? If yes: retire it, don't migrate it.
  2. For new extensions: Does this require transactional consistency with a standard SAP Business Object, bounded to S/4HANA data only? If yes: RAP is correct.
  3. For new extensions: Does this require data from outside S/4HANA, independent scalability, or multi-tenant deployment? If yes: CAP is correct.
  4. Before committing to either: Is RAP on the table because of architectural justification — or because of who is in the room?

Question 4 is the one that changes the outcome. And it takes organizational courage to ask it out loud.

The CAP vs. RAP debate is not a technology debate. It's an organizational clarity debate — and more often than not, an organizational courage debate. The teams that get this right are the ones willing to name what's actually driving the decision, separate ABAP expertise from ABAP default, and deploy both models where they genuinely belong.

References

  • RAP vs CAP - Key Differences between the Two Programming Models — SAP Community
  • CAP vs. RAP: Navigating the Choice of SAP Programming Models — SAP Community
  • ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model — SAP Help Portal
  • SAP Cloud Application Programming Model — SAP Community
  • Five Reasons to Use CAP — qmacro.org
  • Clean Core Extensibility: Balancing Standardization and Differentiation — SAP Community

Walter Aguero Machado —SAP Enterprise Architect | SAP BTP SME at Globant | SAP Certified Solution Architect & BTP Administrator | ASUG Speaker

entry.title: Re: SuccessFactors Learning - Competency Integration with...
entry.content:
Hi @CPat 

Competency related reports like the Competency Assessment (CSV) report are no longer supported in LMS and are not returning data.
Customers should generate Compentency related reports from Report Centers.
Workaround: build a custom report with the competency related table: PA_STUD_CPTY_ASSESSMENT
STUD_ID = user ID
CPTY_ID = competency ID
CPNT_ID = item ID
ASSESS_VALUE = competency rating received by the userCompetency related reports like the Competency Assessment (CSV) report are no longer supported in LMS and are not returning data.

entry.title: Re: SSO Migration from ADFS to Entra ID. IAS – Guidance R...
entry.content:
Hi @SergueiK 
SAP best practices is not to remove ADFS(Since Active Directory Federation Services is included with Windows Server at no additional cost too)

Configure Entra ID as a second Corporate IdP in IAS while keeping ADFS

===================================
The IAS tenant currently has:
Corporate IdP #1 → ADFS
Application IdP → IAS
SuccessFactors + LMS → IAS
===========================
1. Add Microsoft Entra ID as a New Corporate IdP
IAS Admin Console:
Identity Providers → Corporate Identity Providers → Add → SAML 2.0 IdP
For achieve this
i) Download IAS SAML metadata
ii)Register IAS as an Enterprise Application in Entra ID
iii) Upload Entra ID metadata back into IAS
With this you create : Corporate IdP #2 → Microsoft Entra ID
Both ADFS and Entra ID will now coexist.

2. Do NOT switch global IAS default IdP yet
IAS allows you to assign different IdPs per application.
Now you have
• ADFS = default Corporate IdP
• Entra ID = available but not active globally

3) Bind Entra ID only to the LMS Preview tenant first
steps :
i)Log in to IAS Admin Console
Identity Authentication → Applications
You will see a list of all applications connected to IAS (SuccessFactors, LMS Preview, LMS Prod, etc.)

ii) Open the LMS Preview Application
Navigate to: Application → LMS Preview (Test)
iii) Go to Conditional Authentication
Navigate to: LMS Preview → Trust → Conditional Authentication
This is where IAS decides which Corporate IdP to use for this specific application.
iv)  Add a New Rule for Entra ID
Name : Entra ID for LMS Preview
Condition:
Leave empty (implies apply to all users accessing this application)
OR
Add specific test users/groups if you want a phased rollout
Action:
Select Corporate Identity Provider = Microsoft Entra ID
This tells IAS:
“When users access LMS Preview, authenticate them using Entra ID instead of ADFS.”

4. Save and Activate the Rule
IAS processes rules in order, making sure: The Entra ID rule is above any default rule

The default rule still points to ADFS for all other apps
This ensures only LMS Preview is affected.

5. Test end-to-end SSO
Validate: 
Entra ID login flow
User attribute mapping (NameID, email, login ID)
IPS provisioning flows (if used)
LMS deep links
Mobile app (SuccessFactors Mobile → IAS → Entra ID)
Learning admin vs. user roles

6. Communicate & Prepare Production Cutover
Once Preview is stable:
Replicate the same configuration for LMS Production
Switch Production application’s conditional authentication to Entra ID
Keep ADFS available for rollback until fully validated

7. Decommission ADFS (Optional, carry out this step only after stabilization)
Once all SuccessFactors modules and other IAS-integrated apps are confirmed working
Remove ADFS from IAS
Remove ADFS from IPS (if used)
Retire ADFS infrastructure

Decommissioning ADFS is a critical task - the process involves careful verification, uninstallation of server roles, and a thorough cleanup of Active Directory and DNS records
Please refer SAPs best practices on how to retires AD FS
Cheers - Remi

entry.title: Re: Story 2.0 - bookmark date(BW Variable) was not changi...
entry.content:
Dear Signify,

Hope you are well. Thank you for your message. 

For the bookmark in optimized story, please refer to the information in the following help documents first:

Bookmarks in Optimized Story Experience
Restrictions on Bookmarks
Configure Bookmark Settings and Use Related APIs (Optimized) | SAP Help Portal

==>

As for our scenario, could you please kindly check whether this Data Variable is a dynamic variable in BW side?

If yes, you can define whether story viewers’ last changes to the dynamic variable values can be saved with bookmarks via Story Details. For more information, see Set Story Details.

If viewers save a bookmark when you haven’t enabled the option, it might be opened with the last changed variable values of the last opened bookmark after you later set isKeepLastDynamicVariableValue? to true or selected Override default settings and Keep last saved values for dynamic variables.

For the next step, it is also recommended to change this setting in Story Details accordingly. Then, please kindly recreate this bookmark and check whether this issue would still occur.

Thank you for your time.

Best wishes,

Lena

entry.title: Re: Exclude condition types from Profit Margin Calculation
entry.content:
As I already explained, since your surcharge conditions:

  • are customer‑charged amounts
  • are not statistical
  • and sit before PCIP

they will always be included in the profit margin calculation by standard behavior.

Couple of options are
If the surcharge is a true pass‑through cost (freight, tax recovery, regulatory surcharge):

  • Configure the condition purpose as Cost
  • Let it reduce margin implicitly by increasing costs
  • PCIP remains logically correct

This works only if business allows them to be treated as costs

The other option is to go with custom validation with the available BAdIs in Public Cloud

entry.title: Re: Sales Promotions & Condition Contract To Map Schemes...
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, you cannot create new condition types or access sequences for free goods, but sales promotions and condition contracts can still be mapped using the standard “Free Goods Processing (BKA)” scope item. This allows you to configure schemes like “Buy 10, Get 1 Free” within the system, though with limited flexibility compared to on-premise.

Condition contracts are supported in Settlement Management for rebates and promotions. They can be used to manage customer-specific agreements, but customization is limited in Public Cloud. You cannot create new condition types, but you can leverage existing ones for discounts or free goods.

For more information on this. check this discussion  where your query is answered

entry.title: Re: QM Inspection lot: What is the use of Final Delivery...
entry.content:
When you mark "Final Delivery" in QAC1, the system updates the purchase order history to indicate that the item is complete. It prevents further deliveries from being expected in MRP and purchasing. However, it does not technically block additional goods receipts,  you can still post more GRs later if needed.

Why you can still post GR is because, the Final Delivery flag is more of a business indicator than a hard stop. SAP allows subsequent GRs in case of corrections, late deliveries, or business exceptions. If you truly want to prevent further GRs, you would need to close the PO item or set the delivery completed indicator in the PO itself.

entry.title: Re: Extending Standard S/4 HANA OData API's
entry.content:
A nice extensive guide for if you really want to extend the standard API's.
The additions that @junwu made are on point

  • for existing Z fields the SCFD_EUI transaction can be used to handle them as if it were custom fields
    it projects the old z fields as if it are S/4 custom fields, allowing all the  same handling as the S/4 custom fields 
  • If that is not an option manual extension is possible and for that the existing E_ views should be extended as a priority

I just have two more things that come to mind

  • In case it is about adding an existing standard field the option for a Data Source Extension via the so called app should be considered as well
  • When manually extending a CDS based view, always make sure that all the views to be extended are released, if this is not the case it should be considered a big potential upgrade risk
    Personally I never extend a CDS if it is not released, instead I will build my own model with everything on top just to be safer

entry.title: Re: How to remove stock in transit or in transfer
entry.content:
If you don’t want stock to sit in transit, always complete both sides of the transfer (issue + receipt). If stock is stuck in transit due to errors, you may need to reverse the transfer posting and reprocess it.

MB52 only reflects the current state; it doesn’t allow you to directly “remove” stock in transit — you must resolve it through proper goods movement postings.

The way to “remove” stock in transit is to post the receiving GR (movement type 305) if the transaction is on Purchasing side or reverse the transfer if it was incorrect. Similarly, if it is STO, either via 642 to reverse in the supplying plant or 101 to do GR in the receiving plant

entry.title: Re: Requirement of Custom Workflow of Integration of SD &...
entry.content:
You are essentially trying to design a custom MTO (Make-to-Order) workflow with multi-level approvals and departmental hand-offs in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud. Since Public Cloud restricts deep customizations (like custom workflow engines or Z-tables), the solution has to leverage standard workflow capabilities, flexible workflow, and SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) extensions

You can explore the below approaches

  • Flexible Workflow: Use delivered workflow framework in Public Cloud for approvals and notifications.
  • SAP BTP Extensions: For SLA monitoring, escalation, and custom alerts, leverage SAP BTP (Workflow Service + Alert Notification).
  • Fiori Apps & CDS Views: Build dashboards for order status and delay tracking.
  • Escalation Matrix: Configure escalation levels in workflow definitions.

This approach gives you end-to-end visibility, accountability, and escalation without violating Public Cloud restrictions.

entry.title: Discover the Power of Cloud ERP: Bringing Your Business into the Next Software Era
entry.content:
Over the past years, we have witnessed remarkable technological advances: from the rise of Software as a Service (SaaS) to the rapid adoption of artificial intelligence across the software industry. These innovations are reshaping the next generation of business solutions and revolutionising how companies will operate in the decades ahead. 

Today’s technologies empower organization with faster insights, higher efficiency, and new ways to differentiate and innovate. They boost productivity and support better, data driven decisions. 

SAP Cloud ERP: The Next Generation of Intelligent Business Software 

With more than 50 years of industry expertise, SAP is delivering a new generation of business solutions through SAP Cloud ERP. This modern platform combines proven best practices with intelligent technologies to help companies achieve greater efficiency and stronger business outcomes. 

SAP Cloud ERP is a SaaS (Software as a Service) based solution enhanced with embedded AI capabilities. It offers modern and intelligent end to end processes across finance, procurement, sales, service, supply chain, and manufacturing. These processes are delivered as industry best practices, enabling faster implementations and providing a stable, controlled approach for extending the system with custom developments. 

Users benefit from an intuitive, launchpad based experience and the power of Joule, SAP’s intelligent assistant, which accelerates task completion and supports knowledge enrichment. Integrated AI features further enhance daily operations and improve business insights. 

Flexibility to Extend and Innovate 

If the standard end to end processes do not fully meet your business needs, SAP Cloud ERP offers extensive flexibility: 

  • Out of the box integrations with other SAP and third party solutions 
  • Hundreds of APIs available via the SAP API Hub to tailor your processes 
  • SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) to build custom extensions and further innovate according to your unique requirements 

This platform ensures your organization can grow and evolve without compromising stability or/and scalability. 

 How to transform to SAP Cloud ERP  

Customers can start this transformation journey leveraging the SAP integrated toolchain with SAP Signavio Process Insights (optional) and/ or Digital Discovery Assessment – DDA,  focusing either their own historical data or processes or their business processes as a starting point. It is a fact-based approach for the transformation.  

Are you curious to understand more about our next software generation Cloud ERP and how to move into this journey? I welcome you to join us on our next webinar on  April 14, 2026 about Innovation, Scope & Standard Business Processes. You can register here: https://events.sap.com/transformation/en_us/home.html .

entry.title: Re: DRC for France einvoicing B2G and B2B
entry.content:
Why you see FR_EUINV but not FR_B2BINV is because FR_EUINV is part of the EU invoice scenario, which is delivered by default. FR_B2BINV (France B2B invoice) is a newer scenario that requires explicit activation of the France B2B package. If the BC Set for France B2B is not activated, the entry won’t be created in EDOEUDOCTYPE.

So what you should do is Go to EDOC_CUST → Define eDocument Types and check if France B2B is available for activation. Verify that the BC Set for France B2B eInvoicing has been imported and activated. Ensure you are on the latest DRC content version for France. Some document types were only delivered in later support packages. Transport the activation into your target system if you only did it in DEV.

entry.title: Re: Deactive / sell / scrap assets in company codes close...
entry.content:
SAP does not allow hard deletion of assets with postings. The only supported way is archiving (write + delete). If assets remain in ANLA after delete step, check the archiving logs; they will tell you why those assets weren’t removed.

As you might be knowing, the important prerequisites are Assets must be fully retired, transferred, or otherwise closed and No open items or incomplete transactions should exist. 

If you only want to stop errors and exclude assets from reporting, you can flag assets for deletion in master data (transaction AS02 → Deletion flag). This won’t remove them from ANLA, but they won’t be used in processing. True removal requires successful archiving

entry.title: Re: Uploading bulk manual test cases in test preparation app

Hello customer,

If your requirement is to "create" test cases via bulk upload, it's not possible in SAP Cloud ALM at current phase. This is documented in KBA 3209210 - Can Test Cases be created via Excel upload in SAP Cloud ALM? If you wish to influence the future development of SAP Cloud ALM and provide direction to our Developers in relation to this topic, please use the options outlined in this KBA:
3391676 - SAP Continuous Influence for SAP Cloud ALM

If your requirement is to mass "update/edit" test cases via excel template, such as specifying Activities & Actions, Application Name & URL for the Activities, yes you can do this now. For details steps, please refer to https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/excel-upload-of-test-case-into-sap-cloud-alm/ba-p/13547312.

entry.title: Re: Fiscal variant change in Ledger 2L without data
entry.content:
Good day @isabel_martingomez 

Thank you for your question.

What you want to achieve is generally possible only if company code 0062 is still completely clean, meaning no relevant data footprint exists yet.

So, the issue is not the design itself. The issue is that your system is detecting existing data somewhere, even if you did not make any manual postings.

See the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/9dda2d38e0e74490a99dcaf5b5c678f3.html?locale=en-US

If your system says data already exists, then it probably reflects that:

  1. There are already accounting related records for company code 0062
  2. Ledger or accounting principle assignments were already saved in a way that now blocks the change
  3. A technical or configuration consistency check is treating the company code as no longer initial.

I suggest:

  • You check your configuration activity 103556. In company code 0062, check that 0L remains the leading ledger, 2L is the non leading ledger, and the accounting principle assignment is correct.
  • Retry the fiscal year variant change in your configuration activity 105409 for the same company code and ledger 2L: this time, change the fiscal year variant from K4 to V3 and save it.
  • To ensure you do not really have any entries in that company code and to confirm it really empty, do the following:

                 A. Check the Manage Journal Entries and

                B. Display Line Items in General Ledger app. Make sure you filter by the same company code 0062. 

If you find any document, then the system is acting correctly in blocking the change. If no document exist and the error still appears, then it is likely a configuration consistency issue.

If all above mentioned checks are clean and your system still throws the error, raise an incident and include details to help SAP analyse the source.

Here are references for more guidance

3530183 - Assigning Accounting Principles to Ledgers and Company Codes in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: Details on Configuration in General Ledger Accounting and Asset Accounting - SAP for Me

3607050 - Periods of FY variant for ledger & and leading ledger must be same - SAP S/4 HANA Public Cloud - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Subcontracting PO – GR Value Should Remain Fixed Desp...
entry.content:
In standard SAP S/4HANA with Material Ledger active, subcontracting charges are always quantity‑based (GR quantity × PO price). There is no delivered configuration to fix the subcontracting value independent of yield. To achieve a fixed charge model, you must use either a commercial workaround (e.g., service PO or lump‑sum condition) or a technical enhancement (BAdI/user exit) to override valuation logic.

Having said that, below are the options to meet your requirement

entry.title: A Great Combination: SAP Central Finance and SAP Green Ledger
entry.content:
We are pleased to announce the integration of SAP Central Finance and SAP Green Ledger, enabling organizations to leverage sustainability reporting capabilities based on their Central Finance system.

With the release of S/4HANA 2025 FPS01, your Central Finance system can serve as the central hub for providing the necessary financial data for SAP Green Ledger, meaning that it is no longer necessary to connect multiple ERP source systems to SAP Green Ledger and eliminating the efforts involved in separate data consolidation for Green Ledger reporting.

The Central Finance system provides all the data that is needed to enable SAP Green Ledger functionalities such as:

  • Tracking greenhouse gas emissions across the value chain.
  • Monitoring energy consumption and waste generation.
  • Analyzing the environmental impact of products and services.
  • Supporting compliance with various environmental regulations and standards.

 Because SAP Central Finance (CFIN) is always run on an SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition system, the SAP Green Ledger Integration works in the same way as the standard integration with SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition, with some minor differences, which we will outline in this blog.

  

SAP Green Ledger System Landscape 🏡

 

 

As you can see from the architecture above, the mandatory integration deals with the data flows from Central Finance to SAP Green Ledger. These contain master data as well as transactional data.

If you would like to know how to set-up the technical integration between Central Finance (which is always run on an SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition system) and SAP Green Ledger, you can find the details here:  https://help.sap.com/docs/green-ledger/integration/s4hana

 

Further solutions can be used in the landscape optionally/on top, for example

  • SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud: These products allow the consumption of extended reporting with many capabilities (SAP Green Ledger Analytical Dashboard).
  • SAP Sustainability Footprint Management: You can calculate carbon emissions in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management and import these emissions directly from SAP Sustainability Footprint Management into SAP Green Ledger.
  • SAP Group Reporting: You can map SAP Green Ledger data to the SAP S/4HANA Group Reporting table ACDOCU and import greenhouse gas emissions data into SAP Group Reporting for consolidated reporting.

 

Details on Master Data Integration 👨‍

 

The screenshot above shows an overview of the master data objects that are needed in SAP Green Ledger.

The following objects are usually already set-up/mapped in CFIN to run Finance processes:

  • Company codes
  • Chart of accounts
  • Ledgers
  • Accounts
  • Cost centers
  • Profit centers
  • Projects/WBS (optional)
  • Segments (optional)
  • Fiscal year variants
  • Suppliers
  • Companies (optional)

 

Companies

It is sufficient to upload company codes from CFIN to Green Ledger. But if you have already set up companies in CFIN, then these can also be transferred and can be used for Sustainability Reporting on company level. Companies are set up when you use Group Reporting on CFIN, for example.

 

You need to ensure that the following objects are also available in CFIN, although they are more closely related to logistics processes:

  • Plants
  • Material
  • Unit of measure
  • Unit of measure dimensions

 

Please note that a product in SAP Green Ledger is referred to as a material in SAP S/4HANA.

If plants and materials have already been replicated from the source systems to the CFIN system (for example, if you are using product costing), then there is no action to be taken. If the objects are not available, you need to treat them as replication objects, for example, using SAP MDG (Master Data Governance) or your own master data replication tools.

For units of measure and units of measure dimensions there are standard codes pre-delivered on the SAP S/4HANA system.

 

The following objects are new objects in SAP S/4HANA and are also required for Green Ledger:

  • Semantic tags

Semantic tags are used in reports or KPIs defined by SAP. The tags are pre-delivered on SAP S/4HANA. You use semantic tags in the financial statement versions by assigning hierarchy nodes from financial statement versions to semantic tags. This enables the system to calculate values for the semantic tags. The same sorting and structuring by semantic tags is also possible in Green Ledger Reporting.

 

 

Details on Transactional Data Integration 💱

There are various business processes that cause carbon emissions. One example is material movements in the procurement process, such as goods receipt from supplier.

In a Central Finance scenario, material movements are posted in the source systems. The postings are replicated to the Central Finance system and posted in CFIN as Universal Journal Entries.

Green Ledger needs these postings to calculate carbon emissions. The data is posted in Green Ledger as carbon collections.

The two apps above can be used to manage your carbon collections.

In the app “Manage Carbon Data Import” you trigger the import of transactional data from the Central Finance system. For material movements you need to specify certain selection criteria to start the import:

 You also need to upload a valuation file. This is a file which enables data mapping in Green Ledger. In this file, you can specify for each company code which values should be mapped during the import. One example is the GHG category, which is mapped into the emission scope in Green Ledger. There is a template file available, which you can download directly from the app (see screenshot below).

Click “Download Template” to access the template, as shown below:

Then you maintain your values in the file, upload it again and then carry out the import activities.  

 

In the app “Manage Carbon Collections” you can display and edit carbon collections. You can also create carbon collections manually in this app.

 

If you drill down into a carbon collection, you can find the journal entries related to this carbon collection posted in Green Ledger. The carbon emissions are posted with different scopes:

  • Scope 1: Direct emissions
  • Scope 2: Purchased Energy
  • Scope 3: Indirect value chain emissions

The material movements belong to indirect value chain emissions.

 

 

 As soon as the carbon collections are posted in SAP Green Ledger, you can continue with activities in Green Ledger such as

  • Manage journal entries
  • Manage allocations
  • Display account balances in tons of emissions
  • Display line items with emission data
  • Monitor GHG emissions on the Carbon Data Dashboard

 

Green Ledger Process Flow ⏳

Once you have completed the set-up, the full Green Ledger Workflow is supported:

For the complete documentation, see Green Ledger Overview: https://help.sap.com/docs/green-ledger/use/overview

  

Special Considerations for Central Finance 🤔

As already mentioned above, you can connect an SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition system to SAP Green Ledger and you can connect a Central Finance system to SAP Green Ledger. There are no technical differences, but there could be slight differences in the processes and certain points to be taken into account.

Opening Balances

When you go live with Green Ledger for an SAP S/4HANA system, you need to create opening balances for the carbon collections. This is done by manually uploading carbon data via CSV file upload in Green Ledger. After that you can start the import of data from your connected SAP S/4HANA systems.

The same handling is needed for integration to Central Finance. You might ask yourself why the Central Finance initial load cannot be used as a starting point. The reason is that the Central Finance balance load does not take quantities into account. Therefore it cannot transfer the necessary data to calculate the emissions. That means that you need to manually upload balances as usual for an SAP S/4HANA system.

 

Material Ledger

Material Ledger cannot be actively used on a Central Finance system. But this is not an issue. The activation of Material Ledger is not a prerequisite for integration with SAP Green Ledger. The value flows and quantity flows from the source systems to Central Finance contain the data needed in SAP Green Ledger.

 

Material Movements

For every material movement in SAP S/4HANA, there is one Journal Entry posted with carbon emissions in SAP Green Ledger. Material Movements currently cover purchase order, production order and stock transfer related material movements.

In SAP Green Ledger, the Journal Entries with carbon emissions are posted per plant and per product (material). If you need to have the full granularity of emissions per material, you should take this into account when you map materials for Central Finance. In this case an N:1 mapping won’t give you full granularity.

 

Extended Value with SAP Group Reporting 📙

As we already mentioned, SAP Green Ledger and SAP Group Reporting also work together. You can map the data from Green Ledger to the ACDOCU table of SAP Group Reporting and then upload the greenhouse gas emission data to ACDOCU. This enables:

  • Consolidated Reporting and Analysis: Combined reporting of financial and environmental information
  • Enhanced Compliance and Transparency: Meet Regulatory Requirements like IFRS S2
  • Strategic Decision-Making: Identify potential environmental risks and opportunities and track your progress towards sustainability targets 

Please see the details in the Blog: Streamlining Sustainability Reporting: Integrating SAP Green Ledger with SAP Group Reporting

And here comes the extended value:

You don’t have to use a separate instance for SAP Group Reporting, but you can implement SAP Group Reporting on top of your Central Finance system. This means you can also leverage all the benefits of consolidation from your Central Finance system.

 

Ending with an Outlook 👀

There are many innovative topics on the roadmap for SAP Green Ledger.

https://roadmaps.sap.com/board?PRODUCT=73554900100800005432

You can be sure that SAP Central Finance will continue to validate the latest innovations and to enable integration, ensuring that sustainable processes are supported.

entry.title: GitHub Copilot for SAP ABAP in VS Code: Setup Guide for On-Premise Teams
entry.content:
How I Got Here — My Personal Experience

I’ve been working in SAP for close to 20 years now. Over that time, I’ve seen the development landscape evolve from SE38 and SE80 to Eclipse ADT, from classical ABAP to ABAP OO, and now RAP. But nothing has shifted the day-to-day developer experience as quickly as AI-assisted coding.

When our team started discussing AI tools for ABAP development, the first name that came up was SAP Joule. It looked promising, deeply trained on SAP code, integrated into Eclipse ADT, and purpose-built for ABAP. But when I dug into the availability details, I hit a wall: Joule for Developers is not available for on-premise S/4HANA systems. Our landscape is on-premises, and that wasn’t going to change overnight.

So I started exploring what else was out there. I looked at Amazon Q Developer, which has strong ABAP capabilities and an impressive ABAP Accelerator MCP server. But it required standing up AWS infrastructure. Then I checked GitHub Copilot and realized our organization already had Copilot Business licenses under our GitHub Enterprise agreement. The path of least resistance was clear.

I set it up on a Friday afternoon. By Monday morning, I was using it in real development work. The speed at which I became productive surprised me. I honestly expected a longer ramp-up.

What Surprised Me Most

I initially set up Copilot in both Eclipse ADT and VS Code, expecting roughly the same experience. I was wrong. The Eclipse plugin gives you solid code completions and a useful chat panel, but VS Code with Agent Mode is on a different level entirely. The first time I described a multi-step task in natural language and watched the agent plan its approach, create files, and iterate on errors autonomously, I knew this was going to change how my team works.

One moment that stood out: I pointed Agent Mode at a legacy report that nobody on the current team had written. Within minutes, it had analyzed the entire program, generated a functional description, flagged two performance issues with nested SELECTs, and suggested refactored code with JOINs. That single interaction would have taken a developer half a day to do manually.

Another area where I’ve seen real value is unit test generation. Let’s be honest, ABAP Unit tests are often the first thing that gets cut from project timelines. With Copilot’s /tests command, you can generate a solid test scaffold in seconds. It’s not perfect, you still need to review and adjust, but it removes the biggest barrier, which is the blank-page problem of writing test classes from scratch.

The AI Landscape for On-Premise ABAP Teams

If you’re on an on-premise SAP system, here’s where things stand today:

Solution

On-Premise?

IDE Support

Key Consideration

SAP Joule

No

Eclipse ADT

Cloud only (BTP / S/4HANA Cloud)

Amazon Q Developer

Yes

Eclipse, VS Code, BAS

Requires AWS ecosystem

GitHub Copilot

Yes

Eclipse ADT, VS Code

Broad model choice, mature Agent Mode

I went with GitHub Copilot because we already had the licenses. Your mileage may vary, if your organization is on AWS, Amazon Q Developer is absolutely worth evaluating. The important thing is that on-premise ABAP teams are no longer locked out of AI-assisted development.

Eclipse vs. VS Code — What I Found

For AI-powered development tasks, VS Code with Copilot Agent Mode is in a different league.

Eclipse ADT + Copilot: Good code completions, a helpful chat panel, and basic agent support that’s been improving with each release. For day-to-day ABAP work, it’s a meaningful step forward.

VS Code + Copilot: Everything Eclipse has, plus fully autonomous Agent Mode with multi-step planning, terminal integration, and direct SAP system awareness through the ABAP FS extension. This is where I do my complex development work now.

Installation Steps

Here’s the exact setup I followed. The whole process took me about 30–45 minutes, and I was productive immediately after.

Step 1:  Install Visual Studio Code

Download and install the latest version of VS Code from code.visualstudio.com. I’d recommend getting the latest stable release — older versions may not support all Copilot features.

Step 2:  Install ABAP Remote Filesystem Extension

This is the extension that makes VS Code talk to your SAP system. Without it, Copilot has no ABAP context — with it, the AI can see your actual system objects.

  •   Open VS Code and navigate to Extensions (Ctrl+Shift+X)
  •   Search for and install ABAP Remote Filesystem

  •   Click on the extension settings to configure

  •   Click on Abapfs: Remote to add your SAP system

Add the SAP system connection details. I configured both our DEV and SBX systems here. Use placeholder values if you’re just testing the setup initially.

Step 3:  Install SAP Web IDE Adapter for VS Code

This extension enables SAP-specific development workflows within VS Code. It’s a small addition but it rounds out the SAP development experience.

  •   From the Extensions marketplace, install SAP Web IDE Adapter

Step 4:  Install SAP Joule Studio Code Extension

Even though Joule isn’t available for on-premise ABAP, I installed the Joule Studio Code Editor extension anyway — it’s useful if you also work with BTP environments, and it doesn’t interfere with Copilot.

  •   Install SAP Joule (Joule Studio Code Editor) from the Extensions marketplace

Step 5:  Install GitHub Copilot Extension

This is the main event. Install the GitHub Copilot extension and sign in with your company’s GitHub account.

  •   Install GitHub Copilot from the VS Code Extensions marketplace

  •   Sign in using your Company GitHub account and ensure Copilot is enabled

A tip from my experience: if your company uses SSO for GitHub, make sure you complete the authentication through the browser window that pops up. I initially missed this and spent 10 minutes wondering why Copilot wasn’t activating.

Step 6:  Activate ADT Service on SAP System

This is the SAP Basis step. Make sure the ADT service is active in transaction SICF. If your Basis team hasn’t already enabled this for Eclipse ADT, you’ll need to request it.

Step 7:  Connect to the SAP System

Now connect VS Code to your SAP system. This is the moment where everything comes together.

  •   Open the Command Palette (Ctrl+Shift+P)
  •   Run: ABAP FS: Connect to an ABAP system

  •   Select the SAP system to connect

Step 8:  Verify SAP System Connection

Once connected, you should see your SAP system’s object tree in the Explorer pane. When I first saw our $TMP package, Core Data Services, and Source Code Library appear in VS Code, it felt like two worlds merging.

  •   Confirm the connected SAP system appears under ABAP Remote Filesystem
  •   You should now be able to browse SAP objects remotely

Step 9:  Verify GitHub Copilot and Model Availability

Finally, confirm that Copilot is active and check which AI models your organization has approved. In our case, we have access to Claude Haiku, Claude Opus, Claude Sonnet, and GPT models. I primarily use Claude Haiku for quick tasks and Claude Sonnet for complex agent work.

  •   Confirm that GitHub Copilot is active in the editor
  •   Verify that your company-approved AI models are available

Quick Start: Using Copilot for ABAP in VS Code

Once installed, open the Copilot Chat panel (Ctrl+Alt+I) and choose your mode. Here’s how I use each one in practice:

Ask Mode — My Go-To for Quick Answers

I use this multiple times a day. Instead of searching SAP Help or opening a browser, I just ask Copilot directly in the IDE. It’s especially useful for BAPIs and function modules I don’t work with regularly.

“Explain how BAPI_USER_GET_DETAIL works”

“Best practices for exception handling in ABAP OO?”

Edit Mode — For Targeted Refactoring

When I have existing code open and want specific changes, I select the code, press Ctrl+I, and describe what I need. This has been particularly useful for modernizing older code patterns.

“Refactor this to use NEW instead of CREATE OBJECT”

“Optimize this to use a JOIN instead of nested SELECTs”

Agent Mode — Where the Magic Happens

This is the feature that changed my workflow. I describe a complex task and let the agent figure out the approach. It plans, generates, reviews its own output, and iterates. Not every result is perfect on the first try, but the starting point it gives you is usually 70–80% there.

“Create an ABAP class ZCL_BP_VALIDATOR with unit tests for BP number validation”

“Analyze program ZSALES_ORDER_REPORT — generate documentation and flag performance issues”

“Create a complete RAP application for insurance claims with CDS views, behavior definition, and service binding”

Slash Commands I Use Daily

These are quick shortcuts in the chat panel that I’ve made part of my routine:

  •   /explain — I use this constantly on unfamiliar legacy code
  •   /fix — Helpful when I get a syntax error I can’t immediately spot
  •   /tests — My favorite — generates unit test scaffolds in seconds
  •   /doc — Generates inline documentation for methods and classes

Creating and Searching Custom Objects

One thing I didn’t expect was how smooth object creation is directly from VS Code. Enter the ‘>’ command to list options, then select ABAP Create Object.

  •   Select the object type to be created

  •   Enter the object name

  •   Enter the description

  •   Enter the package and transport number to save

For searching existing objects, use the ABAP Search for Object command. The filter option is handy when you’re looking for a specific object type.

Custom Instructions — This Is a Game-Changer

If you set up only one additional thing beyond the basic installation, make it this. Custom Instructions tell Copilot how your team writes code — your naming conventions, your preferred patterns, your performance rules. Without them, Copilot gives you generic ABAP. With them, it gives you code that looks like your team wrote it.

Create a file called .github/copilot-instructions.md under each connected system. You define your rules in plain English, and Copilot follows them automatically across all suggestions.

The instructions I set up for our team enforce:

  •   Use of modern ABAP syntax (7.55+) — no legacy patterns in new code
  •   Our naming conventions (ZCL_*, ZIF_*, ZCX_* prefixes)
  •   Performance-safe data access patterns (CDS-first, no SELECT *)
  •   Domain-specific knowledge for our business context
  •   Under each system, add a folder ‘.github’ and a file ‘copilot-instructions.md’

  •   You can deploy centrally or explore global deployment options with your GitHub Administrator
  •   Below is a sample of the instruction file I created for our team

A personal observation: after deploying Custom Instructions, I noticed the quality of Copilot’s ABAP suggestions improved dramatically. It stopped suggesting CREATE OBJECT and started using NEW. It stopped generating SELECT * and started using explicit field lists. These are small things individually, but across a team of developers, they add up to consistently better code.

How My Team Uses This Daily

Here are the five ways GitHub Copilot has become part of our regular workflow:

  •   Agent Mode for direct development — Writing ABAP code directly into the SAP system. This is the use case that saves the most time, especially for generating boilerplate like data declarations, ALV setup, and standard SELECT patterns.
  •   Documentation generation — Pointing Copilot at legacy code and getting clear, readable documentation. For a team maintaining 15+ years of custom ABAP, this alone justified the setup effort.
  •   Code reviews — Using Copilot to review code for quality, performance patterns, and best practices before peer review. It catches things humans miss when they’re deep in the logic.
  •   Unit test generation — Creating ABAP Unit test scaffolds with /tests. We’ve gone from almost zero test coverage on new code to having at least basic test classes for every new method.
  •   Code modernization — Refactoring older ABAP to modern syntax and OO patterns. This is especially useful during S/4HANA preparation work.

Honest Limitations — What I’ve Learned the Hard Way

I want to be transparent about where Copilot falls short for ABAP, because I think setting realistic expectations is important:

  •   The AI models are not fine-tuned for ABAP. ABAP has far less training data on GitHub compared to languages like Python or JavaScript. Copilot sometimes suggests almost-but-not-quite right syntax, especially for SAP-specific APIs and BAPIs.
  •   Module-specific business logic still needs human expertise. Copilot doesn’t know your company’s custom tables, your specific process flows, or your regulatory requirements. Custom Instructions help, but they don’t replace domain knowledge.
  •   Always review the generated code before activating. I’ve caught subtle issues with incorrect table key definitions and missing authority checks. The AI gets you 70–80% there; the last 20–30% is where your experience matters.
  •   This is a developer productivity tool, not an enterprise transformation platform. It complements the specialized tools organizations use for large-scale migration and conversion programs.

Bottom Line

AI-assisted ABAP development works on-premise today. GitHub Copilot in VS Code, especially Agent Mode, has genuinely changed how I work and how my team approaches development tasks. The setup takes an afternoon, and the productivity gains start from day one.

If your organization has GitHub Copilot licenses and an on-premise SAP landscape, I’d encourage you to try this setup. 

I’d love to hear from others in the community who are exploring AI tools for ABAP development. What’s working for you? What challenges have you faced? Let’s keep the conversation going.

#SAP #ABAP #GitHubCopilot #VSCode #AgentMode #AI #OnPremise #S4HANA #GenAI
